passages.explanation: Jesus stays in Galilee because the Jewish authorities in Judea want to kill him. The Feast of Booths (Tabernacles) is approaching, and his own brothers tell him to go to Judea and show his works publicly — if he's really doing these things, why hide? John notes that even his brothers didn't believe in him at this point. Their advice sounds reasonable on the surface, but it's coming from scepticism, not support.

Jesus tells his brothers his time hasn't come yet. He draws a contrast: the world has no reason to hate them, but it hates him because he exposes its evil. He tells them to go to the feast without him. He's not refusing to go — he's refusing to go on their terms, publicly and on their schedule.

After his brothers leave, Jesus goes to the feast quietly, without drawing attention. The Jewish authorities are looking for him. The crowd is divided — some say he's a good man, others say he's deceiving people — but no one speaks openly about him because they're afraid of the authorities. Halfway through the feast, Jesus enters the temple and starts teaching. The Jewish leaders are astonished that he can teach with such depth without formal rabbinic education.

Jesus says his teaching doesn't originate with him — it comes from God who sent him. He offers a test: anyone genuinely willing to do God's will can recognise whether the teaching is from God or self-invented. A person speaking on their own authority seeks their own glory; someone seeking God's glory is trustworthy. Then Jesus turns the tables: Moses gave them the law, yet none of them actually keeps it — so why are they trying to kill him?

The crowd accuses Jesus of being demon-possessed — they claim no one is trying to kill him. Jesus refers back to the Sabbath healing from chapter 5. He makes an argument from their own practice: they circumcise boys on the Sabbath when the timing falls that way, prioritising the law of circumcision over the Sabbath rule. If that's acceptable, why are they furious that he healed an entire person on the Sabbath? He tells them to stop judging by appearances and judge fairly.

Some Jerusalem locals are confused — they know the authorities want Jesus dead, yet here he is teaching openly and no one stops him. They wonder if the rulers secretly believe he's the Christ. But they dismiss the idea: they know where Jesus comes from, and the common belief was that the Messiah's origin would be mysterious. Jesus responds publicly: yes, they know him and where he's from, but they don't know the one who sent him. He does, because he comes from God.

Some try to seize Jesus, but no one manages to — John says his hour hadn't come yet. Meanwhile, many in the crowd believe in him. Their reasoning is practical: when the Messiah comes, would he really do more signs than this man has done? The Pharisees hear this growing support and send temple officers to arrest him.

Jesus says he'll be with them a little longer, then he's going to the one who sent him. They'll look for him and won't find him, and they can't come where he's going. The Jewish listeners are confused — they wonder if he's planning to go teach among the Greek-speaking Jews scattered across the Roman Empire. They don't understand he's talking about returning to the Father.

On the final and most important day of the feast, Jesus stands up and makes a public invitation: anyone who is thirsty should come to him and drink. He says that anyone who believes in him will have rivers of living water flowing from within them. John clarifies that Jesus was talking about the Holy Spirit, which believers would receive after Jesus was glorified. The Spirit hadn't been given yet at this point.

The crowd splits further. Some say Jesus is the Prophet foretold by Moses. Others say he's the Christ. But others object: the Messiah is supposed to come from Bethlehem and be descended from David — and as far as they know, Jesus is from Galilee. The irony, which John's readers would recognise, is that Jesus was born in Bethlehem. Some want to arrest him, but again no one lays a hand on him.

The officers return to the chief priests and Pharisees empty-handed. When asked why they didn't arrest Jesus, they say simply: no one has ever spoken like this man. The Pharisees are scornful — have any of the rulers or educated Pharisees believed in him? They dismiss the crowd as ignorant. Nicodemus, who met Jesus privately in chapter 3, carefully speaks up: their own law requires hearing someone out before judging them. They shut him down with a sneer — are you from Galilee too? They insist no prophet comes from Galilee.
A brief transition line. Everyone goes home. This leads directly into the next chapter.
